Plastic Note: क्या तेज धूप या जेब की गर्मी से पिघल जाते हैं प्लास्टिक के नोट? जानिए क्या है सच
Plastic Note: अब जल्द ही प्लास्टिक के नोट आने वाले हैं. लेकिन अब सवाल ये है कि क्या प्लास्टिक यानी पॉलिमर के नोट तेज धूप या जेब की गर्मी से नहीं पिघल जाएंगे? यहां से आप इस सवाल का जवाब ले सकते हैं.
Plastic Note: ऑस्ट्रेलिया समेत कई देशों में प्लास्टिक यानी पॉलिमर के नोट चलन में हैं. हाल ही में भारत में भी प्लास्टिक नोटों को लेकर चर्चा तेज हुई है. ऐसे में कई लोगों के मन में सवाल है कि अगर ये नोट प्लास्टिक के बने होते हैं तो क्या तेज धूप, गर्मी या जेब में रखने से ये पिघल सकते हैं? आइए जानते हैं इसका जवाब.
पिघल जाते हैं प्लास्टिक के नोट?
नहीं, प्लास्टिक के नोट गर्मी या धूप में नहीं पिघलते हैं. ये खास तरह के पॉलिमर से बनाए जाते हैं, जो आम प्लास्टिक से काफी अलग और ज्यादा मजबूत होता है. इसे इस तरह डिजाइन किया जाता है कि ये रोज के इस्तेमाल, नमी, बारिश और गर्म मौसम को आसानी से झेल सके.
जेब की गर्मी से होगा नुकसान?
कई लोग सोचते हैं कि अगर प्लास्टिक का नोट लंबे समय तक जेब में रखा जाए तो शरीर की गर्मी से खराब हो सकता है. लेकिन ऐसा नहीं होता. इंसान के शरीर का तापमान करीब 37 डिग्री सेल्सियस होता है, जबकि पॉलिमर नोट इससे कहीं ज्यादा तापमान सह सकता रखते हैं. इसलिए जेब, पर्स या बैग में रखने से इन्हें कोई नुकसान नहीं होता है.
कितनी गर्मी तक सुरक्षित रहते हैं?
पॉलिमर नोट मौसम और तेज धूप में भी सुरक्षित रहते हैं. हालांकि, अगर इन्हें बहुत अधिक तापमान वाली जगह, जैसे जलती आग, गैस स्टोव, इस्त्री या किसी औद्योगिक हीट सोर्स के संपर्क में रखा जाए, तब ये खराब हो सकते हैं. यानी रोज की गर्मी से इन्हें कोई खतरा नहीं होता है.
कागज के नोटों से ज्यादा टिकाऊ
पॉलिमर नोटों का सबसे बड़ा फायदा यही है कि ये कागज के नोटों की तुलना में ज्यादा मजबूत होते हैं. ये जल्दी फटते नहीं हैं, पानी से खराब नहीं होते और इनकी उम्र भी अधिक होती है. यही वजह है कि कई देशों ने इन्हें अपनाया है.
नकली नोट रोकने में भी मदद
प्लास्टिक के नोटों में पारदर्शी विंडो, खास सुरक्षा फीचर और आधुनिक प्रिंटिंग तकनीक का इस्तेमाल किया जाता है. इससे नकली नोट बनाना काफी मुश्किल हो जाता है. यही कारण है कि कई केंद्रीय बैंक इन्हें अधिक सुरक्षित मानते हैं.
क्या है स्थिति?
फिलहाल भारत में बड़े पैमाने पर प्लास्टिक नोट जारी नहीं किए गए हैं. हालांकि, समय-समय पर इन्हें लेकर चर्चा होती रही है. अगर भविष्य में भारतीय रिजर्व बैंक पॉलिमर नोट लाने का फैसला करता है, तो वे भी सामान्य गर्मी और धूप में सुरक्षित रहेंगे. अगर भविष्य में भारत में प्लास्टिक के नोट आते हैं, तो तेज धूप, जेब की गर्मी या गर्म मौसम से घबराने की जरूरत नहीं होगी.

दिल्ली-एनसीआर में मौसम हुआ सुहाना, हल्की बारिश और तेज हवाओं के चलते गर्मी से राहत
By - |22 July 2026 9:32 AM IST
दिल्ली समेत पूरे राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) में मौसम ने एक बार फिर करवट ले ली है। बीते 24 घंटों के दौरान आसमान पर काले घने बादलों का डेरा, कई इलाकों में हल्की बारिश और तेज हवाओं के चलते लोगों को उमस और भीषण गर्मी से बड़ी राहत मिली है।
नोएडा, 22 जुलाई (आईएएनएस)। दिल्ली समेत पूरे राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) में मौसम ने एक बार फिर करवट ले ली है। बीते 24 घंटों के दौरान आसमान पर काले घने बादलों का डेरा, कई इलाकों में हल्की बारिश और तेज हवाओं के चलते लोगों को उमस और भीषण गर्मी से बड़ी राहत मिली है।
पिछले कई दिनों से लगातार पड़ रही तेज धूप और गर्म हवाओं के बाद मौसम में आए इस बदलाव ने लोगों को राहत की सांस लेने का मौका दिया है। हालांकि, जिस तेज और भारी बारिश का इंतजार पूरा एनसीआर कर रहा था, फिलहाल उसकी संभावना कम नजर आ रही है।
भारतीय मौसम विभाग (आईएमडी) के ताजा पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार आने वाले पूरे सप्ताह तक मौसम का मिजाज लगभग ऐसा ही बना रहेगा। आसमान में बादल छाए रहेंगे और बीच-बीच में हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी देखने को मिल सकती है। अच्छी बात यह है कि अगले सात दिनों के लिए किसी भी प्रकार की मौसम संबंधी चेतावनी जारी नहीं की गई है।
22 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 32 डिग्री सेल्सियस और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। इस दिन मध्यम बारिश के साथ सामान्यतः बादल छाए रहने की संभावना जताई गई है। 23 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 24 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। मौसम विभाग के अनुसार सामान्यतः बादल छाए रहेंगे और हल्की बारिश हो सकती है।
24 जुलाई को भी मौसम में कोई बड़ा बदलाव नहीं होगा। अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने की संभावना है। पूरे दिन सामान्यतः बादल छाए रहेंगे तथा हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी देखने को मिल सकती है। इसके बाद 25 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 26 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने की संभावना है।
मौसम विभाग ने केवल सामान्यतः बादल छाए रहने का पूर्वानुमान जारी किया है। 26 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 34 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 26 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। मौसम सामान्यतः बादलों से घिरा रहेगा तथा आर्द्रता 80 प्रतिशत से 70 प्रतिशत के बीच बनी रहेगी। 27 जुलाई को भी अधिकतम तापमान 34 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने की संभावना है। पूरे दिन बादलों की आवाजाही बनी रहेगी और आर्द्रता 80 प्रतिशत से 70 प्रतिशत के बीच रहने का अनुमान है।
मौसम विशेषज्ञों का कहना है कि बादलों की मौजूदगी और बीच-बीच में होने वाली हल्की बारिश के कारण फिलहाल भीषण गर्मी से राहत बनी रहेगी। हालांकि, भारी और लगातार बारिश की संभावना फिलहाल कम दिखाई दे रही है। ऐसे में लोगों को उमस से आंशिक राहत तो मिलेगी, लेकिन मौसम में लगातार बदलाव बना रहेगा।
अस्वीकरण: यह न्यूज़ ऑटो फ़ीड्स द्वारा स्वतः प्रकाशित हुई खबर है। इस न्यूज़ में BhaskarHindi.com टीम के द्वारा किसी भी तरह का कोई बदलाव या परिवर्तन (एडिटिंग) नहीं किया गया है| इस न्यूज की एवं न्यूज में उपयोग में ली गई सामग्रियों की सम्पूर्ण जवाबदारी केवल और केवल न्यूज़ एजेंसी की है एवं इस न्यूज में दी गई जानकारी का उपयोग करने से पहले संबंधित क्षेत्र के विशेषज्ञों (वकील / इंजीनियर / ज्योतिष / वास्तुशास्त्री / डॉक्टर / न्यूज़ एजेंसी / अन्य विषय एक्सपर्ट) की सलाह जरूर लें। अतः संबंधित खबर एवं उपयोग में लिए गए टेक्स्ट मैटर, फोटो, विडियो एवं ऑडिओ को लेकर BhaskarHindi.com न्यूज पोर्टल की कोई भी जिम्मेदारी नहीं है|

articles.title: 22 जुलाई के लिए मौसम का पूर्वानुमान: मध्य वियतनाम में भीषण गर्मी, उत्तरी वियतनाम में दोपहर और शाम के बाद गरज के साथ बारिश। - Vietnam.vn

articles.full_text:
राष्ट्रीय मौसम विज्ञान और जल विज्ञान पूर्वानुमान केंद्र का अनुमान है कि 22 जुलाई को उत्तरी क्षेत्र में दिन के दौरान धूप खिली रहेगी; दोपहर और शाम के बाद छिटपुट गरज के साथ बारिश होने की संभावना है, और कुछ क्षेत्रों में भारी बारिश हो सकती है।
दक्षिणी वियतनाम और अन्य क्षेत्रों में धूप खिली रहेगी; दोपहर और शाम के बाद छिटपुट बारिश और गरज के साथ तूफान आने की संभावना है।
देश के सभी क्षेत्रों के लिए मौसम का पूर्वानुमान।
हनोई : दिन में धूप खिली रहेगी, कुछ इलाकों में गर्मी रहेगी; शाम और रात में छिटपुट बारिश और गरज के साथ तूफान आने की संभावना है; न्यूनतम तापमान 26-28 डिग्री सेल्सियस और अधिकतम तापमान 32-35 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहेगा, कुछ इलाकों में तापमान 35 डिग्री सेल्सियस से अधिक हो सकता है।
उत्तर-पश्चिमी क्षेत्र: दिन में धूप खिली रहेगी, शाम और रात में छिटपुट बौछारें और गरज के साथ बारिश होगी, कुछ क्षेत्रों में भारी बारिश भी हो सकती है; न्यूनतम तापमान 21-24 डिग्री सेल्सियस और अधिकतम तापमान 31-34 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहेगा, कुछ क्षेत्रों में तापमान 35 डिग्री सेल्सियस से अधिक भी हो सकता है।

Cruelly hot days ahead: Japan sounds alert for heat exceeding 40°C
Japan has issued heatstroke alerts across most of the country as temperatures approach or cross 40°C. Authorities have called the extreme heat a disaster as hospitalisations and deaths raise concern.
Japanese cities are experiencing "cruelly hot days", a new meteorological term devised this year to warn people of intense heat above 40°C, with heatstroke alerts issued across almost the whole country.
Three central cities on Tuesday logged scorching heat above the threshold, called "kokushobi" which translates to "cruelly hot day", according to the Japan Meteorological Agency.
On Wednesday, swathes of other areas, including the central hub of Nagoya and communities near Tokyo in the east, were enduring temperatures approaching or exceeding 40°C. The agency has issued heatstroke alerts in 41 out of Japan's 47 prefectures, urging the public -- especially the many elderly -- to take steps to stay cool.
In the rural Tochigi region, north of Tokyo, an 85-year-old man was found collapsed inside the greenhouse of his farm on Tuesday. He was later confirmed dead, a local fire spokeswoman told AFP.
"The greenhouse was extremely hot inside," she said.
The Fire and Disaster Management Agency on Wednesday urged the public to regard the extreme heat as a "disaster".
"Temperatures have been rising since mid-July, and severe heat is expected to continue nationwide," the agency said.
"It is no exaggeration to call this extreme heat a 'disaster' with many people being rushed to hospitals or even dying as a result of heatstroke," it said.
Scientists say human-induced climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme heat events in Japan and elsewhere. Last year, the country sweltered through its hottest summer on record.
Japan's highest recorded temperature was recorded on August 5, 2025 when it hit 41.8°C in Isesaki north of Tokyo.

articles.title: Japan is selling a “human refrigerator” for extreme heat, a walk-in cooling booth that can cool the body - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
As heat waves become more frequent and intense around the globe, new cooling technologies are being developed to keep people safe. While many of us are still stuck with air-conditioners, a company in Japan has come up with a quirky solution to cool you down quickly in extreme heat: the walk-in cooling booth dubbed the “human refrigerator,” which works in just a few minutes. According to reports, Japanese refrigeration and vending machine manufacturer SDRS developed the Do Hiemon Box, designed to help people cool down quickly after long exposure to high temperatures. The booth, which takes its cue from Japan's refrigerated vending machines, is already drawing notice for its practical approach to preventing heat-related illness.
How does this human refrigerator works
According to the report, the Do Hiemon box is a small refrigerated booth, large enough for one person at a time. Inside there is a chair where users can sit comfortably as the cooling process starts. The booth has an internal temperature of 15°C. Once a person is seated, chilled air at 5°C is blown towards the head, neck, shoulders and back—areas important for regulating body temperature. This directed airflow allows the body to cool down quickly and provides almost instant relief from heat. According to the company, it takes as little as five minutes to cool the body, making it a fast option for those exposed to high temperatures outdoors.
Engineered to beat heat stress
The cooling booth is for those suffering the physical effects of extreme heat, including the early symptoms of heat exhaustion. As per the company, spending around 10 minutes inside the booth can lower body temperature significantly and help alleviate the discomfort of prolonged heat exposure. This makes the technology especially useful for employees who spend long hours outdoors, such as those on construction sites, in warehouses, factories, logistics centers and industrial facilities. It is said to help avoid medical expenses related to severe heatstroke.
Energy efficient and easy to move
According to reports, it offers cooling and is also energy efficient. SDRS says its system uses about half the electricity of a conventional spot air conditioner. The booth offers three cooling and airflow settings to customize the level of intensity to your liking. It automatically turns off after 20 minutes for safety to help prevent overcooling.
Is it being used by public
The company claims that they have installed the machine at various public places nationwide since it went on sale in April. Another example is the Maebashi City Hall in Gunma Prefecture, where visitors can use the cooling booth for free.
Cost and availability
It is available in its standard variant and costs approximately 1.5 million Japanese yen or about Rs 9 lakh (pre-tax).

articles.title: Short Spells of Extreme Heat During Pregnancy May Increase Low Birth Weight Risk, Finds UQIDAR and IIT Delhi Study - IndiaMedToday
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Exposure to just three to four consecutive days of extreme heat during pregnancy may increase the risk of delivering a low-birth-weight baby, according to a national study led by researchers from the University of Queensland-IIT Delhi Academy of Research (UQIDAR), IIT Delhi and the University of Queensland.
Published in the peer-reviewed journal Environmental Research Communications, the study analysed 209,266 births recorded across India between 2015 and 2020 to examine how exposure to extreme heat at different stages of pregnancy affected birth outcomes.
The researchers found that exposure to three to four consecutive “sweltering” days during pregnancy was associated with a statistically significant increase in the risk of low birth weight. Heat exposure during the first trimester was linked to a higher risk of preterm birth, while exposure during the second trimester was associated with low birth weight.
The findings are particularly relevant for states such as Andhra Pradesh and Telangana, where prolonged summers are frequently marked by recurring periods of intense heat.
Unlike previous studies that relied on average or peak temperatures, the researchers assessed heat exposure using consecutive “sweltering” days, defined as days when the India Heat Index exceeded the 90th percentile of the local long-term climate. This approach allowed them to better capture the impact of sustained heat exposure during pregnancy.
The analysis showed that each additional consecutive sweltering day during pregnancy increased the odds of low birth weight by about one per cent after adjusting for other influencing factors.
Lead author Vineetha Vincent from UQIDAR said that although the increase associated with each additional day of heat exposure appeared modest, its cumulative impact across India’s large population could become a significant public health concern.
“While the per-day increase is small, it compounds over a long, hot pregnancy and affects a very large population, making it a meaningful public health concern in a country where extreme heat is becoming more common,” Vincent said.
The study also highlighted significant social disparities in heat-related pregnancy outcomes. Mothers with no formal education or only primary education were more likely to have a low-birth-weight baby or experience a stillbirth as heat exposure increased, compared with women who had higher levels of education.
According to Sagnik Dey, Professor at IIT Delhi and co-author of the study, climate adaptation strategies must account for these inequalities.
“The mothers most exposed to extreme heat are often those with the fewest resources to cope with it: the poorest, the least educated and the undernourished. Any adaptation response that ignores this social gradient will miss the women who need protection most,” Dey said.
The researchers said the findings highlight the need to recognise pregnant women as a priority group in India’s heat action plans, particularly as climate change contributes to more frequent and intense heat events. They added that targeted interventions, including improved access to cooling, hydration, antenatal counselling and community awareness, could help reduce heat-related risks during pregnancy.
The findings were published in the peer-reviewed journal Environmental Research Communications.

Japan grappled with dangerously high temperatures expected to surpass 40 degrees Celsius, prompting the government to declare it a 'brutally hot day.' Heat stroke alerts were issued as residents were advised to stay hydrated and indoors. The new 'kokushobi' category highlights the rising occurrence of extreme heat.
Japan is reeling under perilously high temperatures, with forecasts predicting heat exceeding 40 degrees Celsius in several areas on Wednesday, marking it as a 'brutally hot day' as per the government's new designation.
Heat stroke alerts were announced nationwide, as the mercury was set to reach 40 C in Kumagaya and Nagoya, urging residents to stay indoors, stay hydrated, and use air conditioning.
This comes amid a trend of harsher summers, with last year's records reaching 41.8 C and more recent hospitalizations reaching alarming numbers, showcasing the intensifying pattern of extreme weather.

Residents are encouraged to take precautions to reduce the risk of heat-related illness, which can develop quickly during periods of extreme heat and high humidity.
Heat Safety Tips
- Drink plenty of water and stay hydrated throughout the day.
- Stay indoors or in air-conditioned spaces whenever possible.
- Limit outdoor activities, especially during the hottest part of the day.
- Ensure pets have access to fresh water and shade, and avoid walking them on hot pavement.
- Check on elderly neighbors, family members, and others who may be more vulnerable to extreme heat.
- Never leave children or pets unattended in a vehicle. Car interiors can reach deadly temperatures within minutes, even with the windows cracked.
- If you must be outdoors, wear lightweight, light-colored clothing, take frequent breaks in the shade, and watch for signs of heat exhaustion or heat stroke.
Stay informed by monitoring local weather forecasts and following guidance from the National Weather Service.

Extreme heat could cost the Italian economy between €6 billion ($7 billion) and €12 billion ($13.8 billion) annually, equivalent to 0.2% to 0.4% of gross domestic product (GDP), the Italian business association Confesercenti warned on Saturday.
The group said increasingly frequent periods of intense heat are driving up energy costs, reducing productivity, forcing businesses to make additional investments and cutting turnover in sectors most exposed to high temperatures, Italian news agency ANSA reported.
"Living with 30 to 60 days of intense heat every year can weigh on the Italian economy by between €6 billion and €12 billion," Confesercenti said.
The estimate includes the combined impact of higher energy consumption, lower labor productivity, compulsory investments to adapt to rising temperatures and weaker consumer spending.
Confesercenti President Nico Gronchi described extreme heat as "a real climate tax" that has become a structural economic factor.
"It affects investments, productivity, spending and consumer habits, including tourism," Gronchi said.
He warned that the economic impact of rising temperatures could not be addressed solely by individual households and businesses.
"It is a challenge that cannot be overcome by leaving the problem to the initiative of individual families and businesses. Structural interventions are needed," he said.

ALGIERS, July 21 (Xinhua) -- Six people have been killed in wildfires sweeping across northern Algeria as a prolonged heat wave continues to fuel forest and vegetation fires in several provinces, the Algerian Ministry of the Interior, Local Authorities and Transports said Tuesday.
Nassim Bernaoui, spokesperson for Algeria's Civil Protection Department, said 1,460 wildfires have been recorded nationwide since the beginning of July.
Speaking to state radio, Bernaoui said Civil Protection teams have contained between 85 and 90 percent of the blazes through operations conducted in coordination with the army, the General Directorate of Forests, local authorities, other relevant institutions, and volunteers.
Firefighting efforts are continuing to extinguish the remaining fires and prevent them from spreading in the provinces of Annaba, Bejaia, Guelma, El Tarf, Khenchela, Mila, Bouira, Medea and Mascara, he said.
Bernaoui said Algeria has recorded a total of 3,719 wildfires since the beginning of May, compared with 1,501 during the same period last year.
According to the General Directorate of Forests, the fires have destroyed more than 1,666 hectares of vegetation, up from 467 hectares burned during the same period in 2025. ■

How extreme heat is changing life across the South
From higher utility bills to changes in work schedules and outdoor activities, extreme heat is affecting daily life across the South. Experts say the impacts are expected to grow as heat waves become more frequent, longer and more intense. Here's what extreme heat means, how it's affecting communities and steps people can take to stay safe.
What counts as extreme heat?
Extreme heat is generally defined as a period of unusually high temperatures and humidity lasting at least two to three days, with temperatures above 90 degrees.
According to Ready.gov, prolonged periods of extreme heat can affect daily routines and increase health risks. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention warns that heat-related illnesses can become serious and that extreme heat is expected to become more common as the climate changes.
How extreme heat is affecting daily life
Many people are adjusting when and how they spend time outdoors to avoid the hottest parts of the day.
Common changes include:
- Moving outdoor work, exercise and errands to the early morning or evening hours.
- Spending less time walking, biking or using public transportation.
- Driving more often to reduce time in direct sunlight.
- Using more electricity and water to keep homes cool.
Ready.gov notes that prolonged periods of heat can influence everyday habits and routines, particularly during the summer months.
The impact on infrastructure, agriculture and schools
Extreme heat can affect roads, utilities, farms and school activities.
According to Ready.gov and other public sources:
- High temperatures can damage infrastructure, including roads and highways.
- Increased electricity use during heat waves can place additional strain on power grids.
- Heat stress can affect agricultural production by increasing irrigation needs and contributing to crop losses.
- Dairy cows can produce less milk during prolonged periods of extreme heat.
- Concrete and asphalt absorb heat during the day and release it at night, contributing to hotter urban areas.
- Some cities are expanding tree-planting initiatives to help reduce heat buildup in neighborhoods.
- Schools and athletic programs may adjust outdoor activities and practice schedules to avoid the hottest parts of the day.
How to stay safe during extreme heat
Ready.gov recommends taking the following precautions during periods of extreme heat:
- Never leave people or pets in a parked vehicle.
- Stay in air-conditioned spaces when possible or locate a cooling center.
- Drink plenty of water and other fluids to stay hydrated.
- Wear loose-fitting, lightweight and light-colored clothing.
- Avoid strenuous outdoor activities during the hottest part of the day.
- Seek shade when outdoors and wear a wide-brimmed hat.
- Take cool showers or baths.
- Use ovens and other heat-producing appliances less when possible.
- Check on older adults, family members and neighbors.
- Watch for signs of heat cramps, heat exhaustion and heat stroke.
- Make sure pets have access to shade and fresh water. Hot pavement can burn their paws.
As temperatures continue to rise during the summer months, officials encourage people to monitor local weather forecasts and take precautions to reduce the risk of heat-related illness.

The Alpes-Maritimes and Var remain under a yellow heatwave alert, with temperatures climbing to 36°C inland and the hot, dry conditions continuing to increase the risk of wildfires.

TOKYO - A provisional total of 261 people were taken to hospital for suspected heat stroke on Tuesday, according to the Tokyo Fire Department, as a heat wave persisted across the nation.
The figure reflected the daily total as of 9 p.m. but was already the highest for a single day since comparable data became available in 2022, the fire department said Wednesday.
The number of emergency dispatches also reached a record high on Tuesday with a preliminary total of 3,454, the highest since figures were first compiled in 1963.
The number of people reporting heat exhaustion symptoms including fever and headaches also began rising sharply last weekend due to higher temperatures, the department said.
Among the 261 heat stroke emergencies, people in their 80s accounted for the largest portion at 55, followed by those in their 70s, at 53, and those in their 50s, at 27.
According to the Japan Meteorological Agency, the temperature rose to 36.4 C on Tuesday in central Tokyo.
The heat also apparently proved fatal on Tuesday, with an 84-year-old man in the eastern Japan city of Otawara in Tochigi Prefecture dying of suspected heat stroke.
According to the local fire department, the man was found collapsed inside a plastic greenhouse at around 2:15 p.m. Tuesday by his son and later pronounced dead at a hospital.
Wednesday marked the second consecutive day that temperatures reached 40 C or higher in parts of the country, as a high-pressure system continued to cover much of the Japanese archipelago
The heat also hit sports, with a minor league baseball game starting at noon in Saitama, near Tokyo, called off due to extreme heat.
The farm-team game between the Lotte Marines and the Orix Buffaloes was halted and declared a no-game in the middle of the fourth inning, the Marines said.
The weather agency continued urging the public to take measures against heat-related illness.

Falling water levels on the Danube River, driven by persistent heat, are disrupting Serbia's grain exports along the key shipping route.
The Vojvodina Province in northern Serbia, a crucial grain-producing area for the country, has been particularly hard hit. As the Danube shrinks, ships are being forced to reduce their loads to stay afloat, driving up transportation costs and squeezing profit margins for farmers and exporters.
"Today, the water level at the monitoring station in Novi Sad stood at minus 44 centimeters. Normally, it should be around 150 centimeters this time of year. So the river is now roughly two meters lower than usual. This has created significant shipping difficulties. Some sections of the river have become nearly impassable, forcing ships to reduce their loads just to get through," said Bozidar Belos, advisor to the director of public water management company Vode Vojvodine.
Serbia's hydrometeorological department has warned that Danube water levels are expected to fall even further in the coming days.
To address the crisis, Serbia is working with neighboring countries on dredging the river and building sluices and dams to ease pressures on both navigation and irrigation.
Danube water levels plunge amid prolonged heatwave, threatening Serbia's grain exports
Danube water levels plunge amid prolonged heatwave, threatening Serbia's grain exports
An intense heatwave has gripped Egypt, forcing residents to adopt coping measures while disrupting work schedules, tourism and agriculture across the country.
The soaring summer temperatures have driven many Egyptians to seek relief at Red Sea and Mediterranean beaches, where cold drinks and ice cream offer brief respite from the scorching sun. Vacationer Rania said this year's heat feels harsher than usual, prompting her to adjust daily routines.
"It's always hot in the summer but this year is much worse than before. Most of our outings start in the afternoon till late at night. I drink a lot of water and cold drinks, try to avoid direct sun," said Rania. While many Egyptians seek relief indoors or at the seaside, others must continue working outdoors in temperatures that soar above 45 degrees Celsius.
Among them is Aly, a construction laborer in Alexandria, who says the conditions are exhausting despite shorter hours and extra breaks. "We work eight hours a day. It's exhausting. Now we take more breaks than usual to have a drink, tea, water, but we can't take long breaks as we have to finish tasks every day," said Aly Gamal, a laborer.
The heatwave has forced companies to adjust work schedules, cutting hours and adding safeguards to protect employees from soaring temperatures. "It's a commercial residential compound with 150 laborers working each day from 09:00 to 15:00. Last few years we increased the rest hours during the day, we provide more water, shades and umbrellas to protect them from sun and in many cases we tell them to come to work in the evenings only," said Mohamed Shokr, owner of Construction Company.
The mounting heat is taking a toll not only on daily life but also on Egypt's wider economy, disrupting work, power supply and production. A peak in electricity consumption during the summer season has forced the government to increase imports of expensive oil and gas to keep power stations running. The tourism industry has been hard hit, with bookings dropping sharply at open‑air archeological sites in Luxor, Aswan and Cairo. Meanwhile, unfavorable conditions are weakening harvests of key crops such as wheat, rice, fruits and vegetables.
"Less supply of agricultural products raises food prices, hike inflation and this affects low-income earners. The government increases subsidies to the poor to cover the shortages in supplies and price hikes. In turn lower productivity means less income for the state and this obviously adds more burden on the state budget," said Hossam El-Ghayesh, general manager, Osool Holding Company for Investments. In response, the government has introduced measures to ease the impact of recurring summer heatwaves, from cutting outdoor working hours to expanding renewable energy projects.
"The government is trying to address this issue by cutting working hours for outdoor workers in agriculture and construction. It also reduced office working hours for employees to lower electricity consumption especially ACs. At the same time it is establishing new solar and wind energy projects to avoid dependence on costly fossil fuels," he said.

articles.title: Employers in B.C. responsible for protecting workers from heat - CJDC-TV
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
With temperatures expected to surpass 30 degrees across much of the province this week, WorkSafeBC is reminding employers that they are responsible for protecting their workers from heat-related illness.
People working both indoors and outdoors are at risk of developing heat stress and heat stroke, which can be life-threatening.
WorkSafeBC says those working outside, in fields such as construction, agriculture or transportation, are at higher risk.
However, working in kitchens, warehouses or manufacturing can also be hazardous, especially if those facilities don’t have any air conditioning.
Excess sweating, muscle cramps and nausea are all early signs of heat stress, along with weakness, fatigue and dizziness.
If these signs are not recognized and treated early, employees are at risk of developing heat exhaustion or heat stroke. The symptoms of these more serious heat disorders include increased or irregular heart rate, seizures, confusion and decreased consciousness and awareness.
“These illnesses are preventable, and employers have a responsibility to take steps to protect workers, whether they are indoors or outdoors,” said Todd McDonald, head of prevention services at WorkSafeBC, in a news release Tuesday.
WorksafeBC requires employers to conduct risk assessments specific to their workplace, and develop plans to protect workers from the heat.
Employers are encouraged to provide cool drinking water and rest breaks, set up shaded recovery areas and schedule more strenuous activities for cooler parts of the day. They should also rotate workers between hotter and cooler environments, and train them to recognize the early signs of heat stress.
WorkSafeBC accepted 335 heat-related injury claims between 2021 and 2025.

Heavy Rainfall and Thunderstorm Warning
The city is expected to witness continuous rainfall accompanied by thunderstorms and gusty winds, especially in the afternoon and evening hours. Areas near the Western Ghats and low-lying regions are particularly vulnerable to waterlogging and localized flooding.
Impact on Daily Life

articles.full_text:
PUNE: The city and nearby ghat areas saw a sharp rise in rainfall activity on Tuesday, ending a 10-day stretch of mostly light showers. With the shift in weather, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has upgraded its city forecast from “very light to light rain” to “light to moderate rainfall,” with similar conditions expected to continue for the next two to three days.
IMD scientist SD Sanap said Tuesday’s rainfall marked the first significant increase after a relatively subdued phase. “For the past several days, rainfall was largely confined to the very light to light category with only brief spells in between. Tuesday has seen a pickup in activity, and these conditions are likely to continue for around two more days. Moderate rainfall has a fairly wide range, and the city is receiving rain within that expected category,” he said.
Chinchwad received the highest rainfall at 16mm between 8.30am and 5.30pm on Tuesday, followed by Pashan and Lohegaon at 14.8mm each, NDA (14.5mm), Dapodi (14mm) and Shivajinagar (12.9mm). Among other stations, Koregaon Park received 8mm, followed by Hadapsar (7.7mm) and Daund (6mm). In the district, Kurvande in the ghat region recorded 32mm of rainfall, while Bhor received 12.5mm and Malin 8.5mm during the same period.
Sanap said the renewed rainfall activity was driven by a cyclonic circulation over the west-central Bay of Bengal and an associated trough across central India, along with stronger low-level monsoon winds. “A cyclonic circulation persists over the west-central Bay of Bengal at around 3km-5km altitude. An associated trough extends inland from this system, while strong monsoon flow in the lower levels of the atmosphere is also supporting rainfall. These features together are responsible for the increase in rainfall activity,” the IMD scientist said.
The revival was more pronounced across the Western Ghats. During the 24 hours ending Tuesday morning, Bhira recorded 148mm rainfall, followed by Lonavla (off) at 135mm, Shirgaon and Tamhini at 120mm each, Ambone 116mm, Lonavala (Tata) 115mm, Koyna (Navja) 106mm and Dungerwadi 100mm. Several other ghat stations registered between 60mm and 95mm of rainfall, indicating a broader strengthening of the monsoon activity.
IMD has issued red alert for Pune’s ghat areas on Wednesday, warning of heavy rainfall in the region. Pune city, meanwhile, is expected to continue receiving light to moderate rain over the next 48 hours. Sanap advised tourists and travellers to avoid visiting ghat sections over the next 48 to 72 hours due to the likelihood of heavy rainfall. He said rainfall activity across Pune city was expected to remain elevated for at least two more days before gradually easing.

Thought-provoking Opinions, Expert Analysis, In-depth Insights and other Member Only BenefitsThe India Meteorological Department’s orange alert for heavy to very heavy rain failed to bring significant rainfall to Chandigarh on Tuesday, with the city receiving only 1.2 mm rain between 8.30 am and 5.30 pm despite forecasts of enhanced monsoon activity.
The city remained under partly cloudy skies through the day. Intermittent light drizzle and brief spells of rain were reported at scattered places, accompanied by a light breeze, but no widespread or heavy rainfall occurred till the filing of this report.
According to the IMD bulletin, Chandigarh had received 24.4 mm rainfall during the 24 hours ending 8.30 am on Tuesday. However, rainfall activity weakened during the day, with only 1.2 mm rain recorded by 5.30 pm. The city’s cumulative seasonal rainfall increased to 239.8 mm, though it continued to remain 28.4 per cent below normal.
The maximum temperature rose sharply by 2.9 degrees Celsius to 32.3°C after Monday’s significant fall. Despite the increase, it remained 1.5°C below normal. The rise was the second highest in Haryana, after 5°C in Yamunanagar, and the third highest in Punjab, behind Patiala (3.7°C) and Bhakra Dam, Nangal (3.3°C).
The minimum temperature, on the other hand, dropped by 2.4°C to 24.0°C, settling 2.9°C below normal. Relative humidity remained high, ranging between 72 per cent and 92 per cent.
The IMD has downgraded the warning to a yellow alert for Wednesday and Thursday, forecasting fairly widespread rainfall, thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds at isolated places.
No weather warning has been issued from Friday to Sunday, indicating that no major rainfall activity is expected during the weekend.
An IMD spokesperson said the monsoon remained active, but rainfall distribution was expected to fluctuate. “Rainfall during the southwest monsoon is often uneven in space and time. While isolated areas may receive heavy showers, other parts can remain largely dry.

articles.full_text:
The India Metereorological Department (IMD) released satellite imagery of India being wrapped in thick clouds.
IMD issued alerts and advised residents to avoid unncessary travel.
Rain lashed across several districts of Jammu and Kashmir, Maharashtra, Gujarat and Delhi.
The India Metereorological Department (IMD) on Wednesday said in a statement that active monsoon conditions are likely during next 3-4 days in central and adjoining north peninsular India, east and northeast India. This comes as the IMD issued red alert for a few districts in Gujarat- Dadra and Nagar Haveli, Daman, Dangs, Navsari, Surat and Valsad. For Delhi, the IMD statement said, "Fairly Widespread to Widespread rainfall likely over East Uttar Pradesh, Haryana Chandigarh & Delhi and Punjab during 21st-23rd July."
It issued orange alert for districts of several states. Maharashtra's Palghar,Mumbai and districts of Madhya Pradesh that saw an orange alert are: Agar-Malwa, Betul, Chhindwara, Dewas, Harda, Indore, Sehore, Shajapur.
Assam's Charaideo, Dhemaji, Dibrugarh, Golaghat, Jorhat, Majuli, Sibsagar, Tinsukia also saw orange alert. Chamba, Hamirpurr, Kangra, Kullu, Mandi, Shimla, Solan of Himachal Pradesh recieved orange alert from IMD. Chhattisgarh's Gaurela Pendra Marwahi and Korea were given an orange alert.
Orange alert for Mumbai, J&K flooded, Gujarat sees waterlogging
Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) said Tansa Lake, one of the lake supplying water to the Mumbai, started overflowing. The full storage capacity of Tansa Lake is 14,508 crore liters. Grey skies covered Mumbai and rain lashed the city.
Heavy rainfall lashed Bhalesa in Doda district, with swollen rivers, streams, and drainage channels causing damage to houses, crops, and agricultural land. As flash floods, cloudbursts, and landslides continue, the Doda and Kishtwar administrations have urged people to avoid high-altitude, landslide-prone, and riverbank areas until the weather improves, according to ANI.
Valsad in Gujarat experienced waterlogging in several parts. Delhi too experienced rainfall in several parts of the city.
The IMD advised residents of the affected regions to stay alert and take necessary precautions. It also asked the residents to avoid unnecessary travel.
Chamba in Himachal Pradesh saw disruption of daily life due to torrential rains. The IMD had predicted isolated heavy to very heavy rainfall at few places with extremely heavy rainfall likely at isolated places Himachal Pradesh.
Deputy Commissioner of Chamba, Mukesh Repswal, in a conversation with ANI, said, “According to the Meteorological Department's predictions, a red alert has been issued for Chamba district on July 21, and an orange and yellow alert until July 22. Clear weather is expected after that. Rain that began yesterday evening across the district has blocked over 100 roads…machinery has been deployed, and clearance is underway, but the incessant rain is hampering restoration efforts…traffic has been diverted for the time being…rockslides and landslides are occurring continuously on NH-154A, it is currently blocked…I request everyone to please avoid unnecessary travel. Stay away from rivers and streams and refrain from any trekking activities during this time. If you must travel for some reason, travel only during the day; try your best not to leave home after sunset…currently, there have been no reports of any damage to private property or loss of life.”
J&K government issues advisory
Lieutenant Governor of Jammu & Kashmir, Manoj Sinha condoled the deaths caused by the heavy rains, flash floods, and landslides and wished speedy recovery for the injured. He spoke with senior officials and the DCs of Rajouri and Poonch to assess the damage to houses and key public infrastructure. Of the 435 damaged roads, 356 have been restored.
In a post on X, he said, “Teams from the SDRF, NDRF, Army, BRO, NHAI, J&K Police, and Civil Defence are working together, while Quick Response teams are deployed in vulnerable areas.”
Will these showers cover India’s rainfall deficit?
As per the IMD, India currently stands at a rainfall deficit of 21.3%. The monsoon trough- the low pressure belt in India that decides the rainfall- is in normal position. While the northwest and northeast India will experience active monsoon conditions, west, central and south India remain relatively muted.
This year, India experienced the El Niño spell- a climate pattern usually associated with delayed and weaker monsoons. El Niño refers to unusually warm sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean, which often delay the southwest monsoon and reduce the number of rainy days over India.
